mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover Evoque TD4 / SD4 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 2.0 Ingenium diesel engine typically costs £2,800–£4,000 supply only. A fully fitted replacement from a specialist costs approximately £4,500–£6,000. Main dealer new engines start from £8,000–£10,000+ supply only.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Evoque 2.0 TD4 / SD4 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: No — the 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel has a very poor reliability reputation. The most critical issue is timing chain stretch, which can occur between 60,000–100,000 miles. The root cause is oil dilution from incomplete DPF regenerations — a fundamental design issue because the Evoque's DPF is too far from the engine to stay hot enough. Turbo failure is also common.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Evoque TD4 / SD4?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Timing chain stretch — the most critical and costly failure, (2) Oil dilution from DPF regeneration — the root cause of chain failure, and (3) Turbocharger failure — with siren-like whining as the key warning sign.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an Evoque TD4 / SD4 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £8,000–£15,000 Evoque, a £4,500–£6,000 recon is 30–75% of the car's value. For a clean example with good body and no other issues, replacement may be worthwhile. However, for higher-mileage examples under £6,000, evaluate whether the replacement cost exceeds our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Evoque engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.0 Ingenium diesel uses the 204DTD (AJ200D) engine code. TD4 variants produce 150PS (110kW) with 380Nm of torque; SD4 variants produce 180PS (132kW) with 430Nm of torque. The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent timing chain failure on the 2.0 Ingenium diesel?

acceptedAnswer.text: Prevention is not guaranteed, but you can significantly reduce the risk: (1) Change oil every 5,000–10,000 miles with the correct JLR-spec oil, (2) Avoid short urban journeys — regular motorway runs allow DPF regeneration to complete, (3) Regularly check the dipstick for rising oil level or diesel smell (oil dilution warning), and (4) Never ignore cold-start rattle — book an inspection immediately if you hear it. Even with perfect maintenance, premature chain stretch remains a risk.

eraMap.title: Land Rover Range Rover Evoque TD4 / SD4 — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 2.0-litre four-cylinder Ingenium diesel — TD4 (150–180PS) or SD4 (180PS). Notorious for timing chain stretch, oil dilution and turbo failure

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Evoque TD4 / SD4 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an Evoque TD4/SD4 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The transverse engine layout adds complexity.

cta.label: → Compare Evoque TD4 / SD4 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (Catastrophic)
problems.symptoms: Metallic rattle or knocking on cold start, rough running, engine warning light, camshaft correlation faults, loss of power
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles — can occur as early as 60,000 km

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle on cold start, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: This is the single most serious and costly issue affecting the 2.0 Ingenium diesel. The timing chain sits at the rear of the engine, making access extremely difficult. The chain stretches and the plastic guides break, shedding particles that block the oil pickup and cause spun bearings. If the chain snaps, pistons strike valves — full engine replacement is the only fix. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. A £1,200–£2,500 preventative chain replacement is dramatically cheaper than a £4,500–£7,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Oil Dilution from DPF Regeneration (Root Cause)
problems.symptoms: Rising oil level on dipstick, diesel smell in oil, DPF warning light, 'service required' dash warning
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — short urban journeys accelerate the issue
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed early)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice rising oil level or diesel smell

problems.recommendation: Oil dilution from incomplete DPF regenerations is the root cause of many timing chain failures. The Evoque's DPF is positioned too far from the engine to stay hot enough to work properly. Incomplete regenerations allow diesel fuel to seep into the sump, thinning the oil and reducing its ability to protect bearings and guides. If you primarily drive short urban journeys, factor in regular motorway runs to allow passive DPF regenerations. Some owners recommend oil changes every 5,000 miles as a preventative measure.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil dilution guide

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure
problems.symptoms: Siren-like whining or whistling from the engine bay, loss of power, engine management light, black smoke

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear siren-like noise

problems.recommendation: The turbocharger on the 2.0 Ingenium diesel is a common failure point. The turbo can fail as a direct cause of engine failure or as a secondary result. A failed turbo can cause hydro-lock by allowing excessive oil into the intake, bending conrods. If you hear a siren-like whining or whistling, or experience loss of power, book an inspection immediately. A £1,500–£2,000 turbo replacement is far cheaper than the engine damage that follows if ignored.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbo failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Evoque TD4 / SD4 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Full engine replacement is the only reliable fix
Regular oil changes and DPF regeneration cycles
Viable if caught before engine damage occurs
Viable — but check for piston damage

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Evoque TD4 / SD4? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with frequent oil changes (5,000–10,000-mile intervals)
* Evidence of regular motorway use (DPF health)
* No cold-start rattle — this is the critical warning sign
* Extended warranty in place — this engine is high-risk
avoidIf: * Any cold-start rattle — walk away, this is a ticking time bomb
* Rising oil level or diesel smell on dipstick (oil dilution)
* DPF warning lights or 'service required' messages — suggests short-trip usage
* No service history — the Ingenium is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel in the facelift Evoque is one of the most problematic modern engines in the JLR lineup. In What Car's Reliability Survey, the Evoque finished 44th out of 46 cars in the family SUV category. Timing chain stretch, oil dilution, and turbo failure are all well-documented and costly. The fundamental DPF design issue — the filter is too far from the engine to stay hot enough — makes this engine unforgiving of urban driving patterns. Buy with full service history, evidence of regular motorway use, and budget for preventative maintenance. Without this? Walk away — the risk of a £5,000+ engine replacement is simply too high.

cta.label: → Compare Evoque TD4 / SD4 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Evoque with the 2.0 TD4 or SD4 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or knocking from the rear of the engine (timing chain warning — this is critical)
* Dipstick Check — Smell for diesel (oil dilution); check for rising oil level
* DPF History — Ask about driving patterns; short urban journeys are a red flag
* Service History — Including frequent oil changes — 5,000–10,000-mile intervals are recommended
* Turbo Noise — Listen for siren-like whining or whistling under acceleration
* Engine Warning Light — Any warning lights? Have codes been read? Look for camshaft correlation faults
* Smoke Check — Black smoke (turbo) or white smoke (injector / DPF issues)
* Warranty Status — Extended warranty is strongly recommended
